narrative: Dear Alex, imagine the admissions team looking at your application as a story of your journey toward becoming a lawyer, piecing together the chapters from your undergrad days through law school to predict if you'd conquer the bar exam. Your story starts strong with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.2 and an impressive LSAT score of 35, but the model saw some tougher hurdles ahead that tipped the scales toward a 51.3% chance of not passing the bar. The biggest chapter influencing this was your Year 3 decile of 3.0, which stood out as the strongest signal pulling toward failure, suggesting challenges in sustaining top performance later in law school. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile of 4.0, reinforcing a pattern that made the model worry about building momentum. Your LSAT, while high, didn't quite offset those deciles in the model's eyes, acting as another notable push toward struggle. Even your upper-middle family income, full-time study commitment, white racial background, and male gender—all factors that sometimes help—unfortunately aligned in a way that the model interpreted as adding to the risk. Your undergrad GPA of 3.2 played a supporting role in this narrative, not strong enough to counterbalance the rest. Together, these elements painted a picture of someone with potential but a higher likelihood of stumbling at the bar, leading to the tough call to reject your application. It's not the end of your story, though—many rewrite their paths with targeted improvements. What if you focused on boosting those decile rankings in future efforts? You've got the drive; now channel it strategically. We're rooting for your next chapter.
